key_insight: Under heavy-tailed stochastic gradients, non-Euclidean matrix methods like Muon can achieve optimal sample complexity for nuclear-norm stationarity without the extra dimension-dependent penalty seen in Euclidean methods.
engineering_relevance: For large-model training, this suggests matrix-geometry optimizers may be more sample-efficient and robust to noisy gradients than standard Euclidean first-order updates.
method: Models non-convex optimization with bounded p-th central moments for gradients, p in (1,2].
Proves dimension-free optimality for certain non-Euclidean methods under stronger stationarity notions.
Shows Muon reaches an ε-stationary point in nuclear norm with complexity scaling as O(min{m,n}·Δ1L/ε²·(σ/ε)^{p/(p−1)}).
evidence_signal: The abstract reports supporting experiments on large language models and a matching theoretical lower bound showing the sample complexity is optimal for all first-order methods under nuclear-norm stationarity.
watch_item: The advantage is tied to nuclear-norm/Schatten-type geometry and heavy-tailed noise assumptions, so gains may weaken if the gradient noise is closer to light-tailed or the stationarity metric is mismatched.
paper_title: Free Heavy-Tailed Lunch for Muon: A Theoretical Justification of Empirical Success
